input.content:
# sw-cor24-assembler

A self-hosting COR24 assembler written in COR24 assembly.

## Status

**Relaunch saga in progress.** The project was re-scoped away from a
C implementation to a `.s`-only implementation that will eventually
assemble its own source on COR24 hardware. Current milestone:
single-mnemonic smoke test — `sw-as24` reads `nop` on UART, emits
the byte-identical encoding that the Rust cross-assembler would
produce.

See [`docs/plan.md`](docs/plan.md) for the saga breakdown.

## Quickstart

```bash
just vendor-fetch    # materialize vendor/sw-em24/<v>/bin/cor24-run
                     # from the sibling cor24-rs repo (or SW_EM24_BIN
                     # override, or system PATH -- see vendor/active.env)
just build           # assemble src/sw-as24.s -> build/sw-as24.bin
just test            # smoke test: cor24-run's and sw-as24's output
                     # on tests/smoke/nop.s are byte-identical
just --list          # discover every recipe
```

Requirements on the host: `bash`, `just`, `jq`, `xxd` (ships in
`vim-common` on most distros). For `just vendor-fetch` to build
from source, also `cargo` + `rustc` and a clone of
[cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) at
`../cor24-rs`. See the fallback options documented in
[`vendor/active.env`](vendor/active.env).

## Repository layout

```
sw-cor24-assembler/
├── README.md                # this file
├── justfile                 # build / test / vendor recipes
├── docs/
│   ├── prd.md               # product requirements (long-lived)
│   ├── architecture.md      # system architecture (long-lived)
│   ├── design.md            # current-saga design decisions
│   ├── plan.md              # saga roadmap
│   └── utility.md           # forward-looking sw-hexload sketch
├── src/
│   └── sw-as24.s            # the self-hosted assembler (grows each saga)
├── tests/
│   └── smoke/nop.s          # byte-identical smoke test input
├── scripts/
│   ├── vendor-fetch.sh      # fetch vendored tools from manifests
│   ├── build.sh             # assemble sw-as24.s via vendored cor24-run
│   ├── test.sh              # run the smoke test pipeline
│   └── hex2bin.sh           # host-side decoder for sw-as24's hex output
├── vendor/
│   ├── active.env           # single-source-of-truth version pins
│   └── sw-em24/<v>/         # vendored cor24-run (binary is gitignored)
├── .agentrail/              # saga tracking (see docs/plan.md)
└── .agentrail-archive/      # prior C-era saga, preserved for reference
```

## Documentation

- [PRD](docs/prd.md) — goals, non-goals, success criteria, stakeholders.
- [Architecture](docs/architecture.md) — pipeline, data model, open
  questions (hosting model, static size limits, self-host verification).
- [Design](docs/design.md) — current-saga directory layout, vendor
  strategy, justfile recipe set, smoke-test spec, hex-encoded UART
  output rationale.
- [Plan](docs/plan.md) — saga roadmap and change log.
- [Utility](docs/utility.md) — forward-looking design for
  `sw-hexload`, the device-side companion to sw-as24's hex output.

## Cross-repo context

- [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) — the stabilized
  upstream that ships `cor24-run` (combined Rust cross-assembler +
  emulator) and the reference assembler library this project ports
  from. Vendored by pinned commit in
  [`vendor/sw-em24/v0.1.0/version.json`](vendor/sw-em24/v0.1.0/version.json).
- [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator)
  — sw-embed fork of `cor24-rs` reserved for future divergent changes.
  No tracked divergence yet; we vendor from `cor24-rs` directly.
- [sw-cor24-x-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler)
  — Rust cross-assembler library (rlib) used as the behavioural
  reference. Included transitively via `cor24-run`.
- [sw-cor24-project](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project) —
  the ecosystem umbrella listing every COR24 repo.

## What is a "COR24 assembler"?

COR24 is a 24-bit RISC ISA (MakerLisp-inspired). `cor24-run` is the
Rust host tool that assembles `.s` files into machine code and/or
runs them on the emulator. This project's goal is to produce an
assembler that can run on *COR24 itself* — byte-identical output
to `cor24-run --assemble`, with no host dependencies once the
toolchain is self-hosted.

Full statement of goals and success criteria: [`docs/prd.md`](docs/prd.md).

4

6

8	produces machine code. Written in C, compiled by the cross C compiler
9	(`sw-cor24-x-tinyc` / `tc24r`), and runs natively on COR24 hardware.
10

16

18	|------|------|-----------|---------|

# sw-cor24-assembler

Native COR24 assembler written in C — runs directly on COR24 FPGA hardware.

## Overview

A two-pass assembler that processes COR24 `.s` assembly source and
produces machine code. Written in C, compiled by the cross C compiler
(`sw-cor24-x-tinyc` / `tc24r`), and runs natively on COR24 hardware.

This is a key component of the self-hosted development environment:
with a native assembler on COR24, all assembly-based toolchains
(Forth, p-code VM, etc.) can be built on-device without a host PC.

## Naming Convention

| Repo | Role | Written in | Runs on |
|------|------|-----------|---------|
| `sw-cor24-x-assembler` | Cross-assembler | Rust | Host (x86/ARM) |
| `sw-cor24-assembler` | Native assembler | C | COR24 FPGA |

The `x-` prefix denotes cross-tools that run on a host machine.
The plain name is the native tool that runs on COR24 hardware.

## Bootstrapping

```
sw-cor24-x-tinyc (Rust)  compiles  cas24.c  →  cas24.s
sw-cor24-x-assembler (Rust)  assembles  cas24.s  →  cas24.bin
cas24.bin runs on COR24 FPGA  →  native assembler available on-device
```

## Status

In development. See `.agentrail/saga.toml` for implementation progress.

## Related Repos

- [sw-cor24-x-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler) — Rust cross-assembler (reference implementation)
- [sw-cor24-x-tinyc](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-tinyc) — Rust cross C compiler (used to compile this)
- [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator) — COR24 emulator + ISA definitions

## License

See [LICENSE](LICENSE).

input.content:
# sw-cor24-assembler

A self-hosting COR24 assembler written in COR24 assembly.

## Status

**Relaunch saga in progress.** The project was re-scoped away from a
C implementation to a `.s`-only implementation that will eventually
assemble its own source on COR24 hardware. Current milestone:
single-mnemonic smoke test — `sw-as24` reads `nop` on UART, emits
the byte-identical encoding that the Rust cross-assembler would
produce.

See [`docs/plan.md`](docs/plan.md) for the saga breakdown.

## Quickstart

```bash
just vendor-fetch    # materialize vendor/sw-em24/<v>/bin/cor24-run
                     # from the sibling cor24-rs repo (or SW_EM24_BIN
                     # override, or system PATH -- see vendor/active.env)
just build           # assemble src/sw-as24.s -> build/sw-as24.bin
just test            # smoke test: cor24-run's and sw-as24's output
                     # on tests/smoke/nop.s are byte-identical
just --list          # discover every recipe
```

Requirements on the host: `bash`, `just`, `jq`, `xxd` (ships in
`vim-common` on most distros). For `just vendor-fetch` to build
from source, also `cargo` + `rustc` and a clone of
[cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) at
`../cor24-rs`. See the fallback options documented in
[`vendor/active.env`](vendor/active.env).

## Repository layout

```
sw-cor24-assembler/
|-- README.md                # this file
|-- justfile                 # build / test / vendor recipes
|-- docs/
|   |-- prd.md               # product requirements (long-lived)
|   |-- architecture.md      # system architecture (long-lived)
|   |-- design.md            # current-saga design decisions
|   |-- plan.md              # saga roadmap
|   `-- utility.md           # forward-looking sw-hexload sketch
|-- src/
|   `-- sw-as24.s            # the self-hosted assembler (grows each saga)
|-- tests/
|   `-- smoke/nop.s          # byte-identical smoke test input
|-- scripts/
|   |-- vendor-fetch.sh      # fetch vendored tools from manifests
|   |-- build.sh             # assemble sw-as24.s via vendored cor24-run
|   |-- test.sh              # run the smoke test pipeline
|   `-- hex2bin.sh           # host-side decoder for sw-as24's hex output
|-- vendor/
|   |-- active.env           # single-source-of-truth version pins
|   `-- sw-em24/<v>/         # vendored cor24-run (binary is gitignored)
|-- .agentrail/              # saga tracking (see docs/plan.md)
`-- .agentrail-archive/      # prior C-era saga, preserved for reference
```

## Documentation

- [PRD](docs/prd.md) -- goals, non-goals, success criteria, stakeholders.
- [Architecture](docs/architecture.md) -- pipeline, data model, open
  questions (hosting model, static size limits, self-host verification).
- [Design](docs/design.md) -- current-saga directory layout, vendor
  strategy, justfile recipe set, smoke-test spec, hex-encoded UART
  output rationale.
- [Plan](docs/plan.md) -- saga roadmap and change log.
- [Utility](docs/utility.md) -- forward-looking design for
  `sw-hexload`, the device-side companion to sw-as24's hex output.

## Cross-repo context

- [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) -- the stabilized
  upstream that ships `cor24-run` (combined Rust cross-assembler +
  emulator) and the reference assembler library this project ports
  from. Vendored by pinned commit in
  [`vendor/sw-em24/v0.1.0/version.json`](vendor/sw-em24/v0.1.0/version.json).
- [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator)
  -- sw-embed fork of `cor24-rs` reserved for future divergent changes.
  No tracked divergence yet; we vendor from `cor24-rs` directly.
- [sw-cor24-x-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler)
  -- Rust cross-assembler library (rlib) used as the behavioural
  reference. Included transitively via `cor24-run`.
- [sw-cor24-project](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project) --
  the ecosystem umbrella listing every COR24 repo.

## What is a "COR24 assembler"?

COR24 is a 24-bit RISC ISA (MakerLisp-inspired). `cor24-run` is the
Rust host tool that assembles `.s` files into machine code and/or
runs them on the emulator. This project's goal is to produce an
assembler that can run on *COR24 itself* -- byte-identical output
to `cor24-run --assemble`, with no host dependencies once the
toolchain is self-hosted.

Full statement of goals and success criteria: [`docs/prd.md`](docs/prd.md).

## Overview

A two-pass assembler that processes COR24 `.s` assembly source and
produces machine code. Written in C, compiled by the cross C compiler
(`sw-cor24-x-tinyc` / `tc24r`), and runs natively on COR24 hardware.

This is a key component of the self-hosted development environment:
with a native assembler on COR24, all assembly-based toolchains
(Forth, p-code VM, etc.) can be built on-device without a host PC.

## Naming Convention

| Repo | Role | Written in | Runs on |
|------|------|-----------|---------|
| `sw-cor24-x-assembler` | Cross-assembler | Rust | Host (x86/ARM) |
| `sw-cor24-assembler` | Native assembler | C | COR24 FPGA |

The `x-` prefix denotes cross-tools that run on a host machine.
The plain name is the native tool that runs on COR24 hardware.

```

content.content:
- Run: markdown-checker -f <file> -v
     - Exit code 0 = pass, 1 = violations found, 2 = error
     - Check stdout for detailed violation reports with line/column numbers
     - Always preview first: markdown-checker -f <file> --dry-run
     - If dry-run succeeds, apply fix: markdown-checker -f <file> --fix
     - If --fix fails with 'non-fixable violations', the file contains:
     - Use glob patterns: markdown-checker -f '**/*.md' --fix
     - The tool processes files sequentially and reports each result
     - Overall exit code reflects success/failure of all files
     - Tree symbols (├ └ │ ─) → ASCII (+, |, -)
     - Checkmarks/X marks (✓ ✗ ✅ ❌) → [x]
     - Arrows (→ ← ⇒) → ->, <-, etc.
     - Accented letters (é ñ ç) → plain ASCII (e n c)
     - Smart quotes (curly quotes) → standard quotes
     - Math operators (≥ ≤ ≠) → >=, <=, !=
     - Special symbols (© ® ™ …) → (c), (R), (TM), ...
     - Exit code 2 = file not found, permission denied, or invalid arguments
     - Exit code 1 = validation failed (violations present)
     - Exit code 0 = success (valid or successfully fixed)
     - Always check stderr for error messages and warnings

just --list          # discover every recipe
```

Requirements on the host: `bash`, `just`, `jq`, `xxd` (ships in
`vim-common` on most distros). For `just vendor-fetch` to build
from source, also `cargo` + `rustc` and a clone of
[cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) at
`../cor24-rs`. See the fallback options documented in
[`vendor/active.env`](vendor/active.env).

## Repository layout

```
sw-cor24-assembler/
|-- README.md                # this file
|-- justfile                 # build / test / vendor recipes
|-- docs/
|   |-- prd.md               # product requirements (long-lived)
|   |-- architecture.md      # system architecture (long-lived)
|   |-- design.md            # current-saga design decisions
|   |-- plan.md              # saga roadmap
|   `-- utility.md           # forward-looking sw-hexload sketch
|-- src/
|   `-- sw-as24.s            # the self-hosted assembler (grows each saga)
|-- tests/
|   `-- smoke/nop.s          # byte-identical smoke test input
|-- scripts/
|   |-- vendor-fetch.sh      # fetch vendored tools from manifests
|   |-- build.sh             # assemble sw-as24.s via vendored cor24-run
|   |-- test.sh              # run the smoke test pipeline
|   `-- hex2bin.sh           # host-side decoder for sw-as24's hex output
|-- vendor/
|   |-- active.env           # single-source-of-truth version pins
|   `-- sw-em24/<v>/         # vendored cor24-run (binary is gitignored)
|-- .agentrail/              # saga tracking (see docs/plan.md)
`-- .agentrail-archive/      # prior C-era saga, preserved for reference
```

## Documentation

- [PRD](docs/prd.md) -- goals, non-goals, success criteria, stakeholders.
- [Architecture](docs/architecture.md) -- pipeline, data model, open
  questions (hosting model, static size limits, self-host verification).
- [Design](docs/design.md) -- current-saga directory layout, vendor
  strategy, justfile recipe set, smoke-test spec, hex-encoded UART
  output rationale.
- [Plan](docs/plan.md) -- saga roadmap and change log.
- [Utility](docs/utility.md) -- forward-looking design for
  `sw-hexload`, the device-side companion to sw-as24's hex output.

## Cross-repo context

- [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) -- the stabilized
  upstream that ships `cor24-run` (combined Rust cross-assembler +
  emulator) and the reference assembler library this project ports
  from. Vendored by pinned commit in
  [`vendor/sw-em24/v0.1.0/version.json`](vendor/sw-em24/v0.1.0/version.json).
- [sw-cor24-emulator](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator)
  -- sw-embed fork of `cor24-rs` reserved for future divergent changes.
  No tracked divergence yet; we vendor from `cor24-rs` directly.
- [sw-cor24-x-assembler](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler)
  -- Rust cross-assembler library (rlib) used as the behavioural
  reference. Included transitively via `cor24-run`.
- [sw-cor24-project](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project) --
  the ecosystem umbrella listing every COR24 repo.

## What is a "COR24 assembler"?

COR24 is a 24-bit RISC ISA (MakerLisp-inspired). `cor24-run` is the
Rust host tool that assembles `.s` files into machine code and/or
runs them on the emulator. This project's goal is to produce an
assembler that can run on *COR24 itself* -- byte-identical output
to `cor24-run --assemble`, with no host dependencies once the
toolchain is self-hosted.

Full statement of goals and success criteria: [`docs/prd.md`](docs/prd.md).

lines: **Relaunch saga in progress.** The project was re-scoped away from a
C implementation to a `.s`-only implementation that will eventually
assemble its own source on COR24 hardware. Current milestone:
-single-mnemonic smoke test — `sw-as24` reads `nop` on UART, emits
+single-mnemonic smoke test -- `sw-as24` reads `nop` on UART, emits
the byte-identical encoding that the Rust cross-assembler would

attachment.snippet:
1	# Utility -- Device-side hex-to-binary loader
2	
3	**Status:** Forward-looking design sketch (not in the Relaunch saga)
4	**Scope:** Long-lived reference. Updated as the companion utility
5	becomes concrete in later sagas.
6	
7	## Why a utility at all?
8	
9	`sw-as24` emits its machine-code output as printable hex ASCII on
10	UART TX (two characters per byte). `docs/design.md` Section "Why
11	hex-encoded UART output" covers the drivers: transport robustness,
12	human-debuggability, and a sidestep of cor24-run's 0x00 display
13	filter. That encoding choice is great for *observation* and for
14	*shipping bytes over a text channel*, but a text string is not
15	executable. Something has to turn `"FF"` back into the byte
16	`0xFF` before the CPU can branch to it.
17	
18	During development today, the something is `scripts/hex2bin.sh` --
19	a host-side shell script that wraps `xxd -r -p`. That works when
20	a developer is driving from a laptop with `cor24-run` and UART,
21	but it assumes there *is* a host. Once COR24 hardware is
22	self-hosted (a monitor with a shell, a resident editor, a
23	scripting language), the host disappears. The decoder has to
24	move on-device.
25	
26	## The pair
27	
28	```
29	   sw-as24               (future) hex-loader
30	      |                           |
31	      v                           v
32	   +------+   UART / pipe    +----------+   raw bytes   +---------+
33	   | src  | ---------------> |  decode  | ------------> | memory  |
34	   | .s   |  "FF" "00" ...   |   "FF"   |   0xFF ...    |  buffer |
35	   +------+                  |  -> 0xFF |               +---------+
36	                             +----------+                    |
37	                                                     (jmp to start addr)
38	```
39	
40	Two programs, same data, two stages:
41	
42	| Stage            | Input               | Output                        | Where it runs                |
43	| ---------------- | ------------------- | ----------------------------- | ---------------------------- |
44	| sw-as24          | `.s` text on UART   | hex-ASCII stream on UART      | COR24 (cross-assembled today, self-hosted later) |
45	| hex-loader       | hex-ASCII stream    | bytes in memory at `&dest`    | COR24 (always on-device)     |
46	| (optional) exec  | bytes at `&dest`    | control transfer              | COR24                        |
47	
48	The hex-loader is also usable standalone: any program or user that
49	can type or paste hex on UART can have those bytes appear in memory
50	at a chosen address. That pattern -- paste-to-execute -- is a
51	classical monitor feature on small systems (e.g., ROM monitors on
52	1970s/80s microcomputers), so the utility earns its keep beyond
53	the sw-as24 pairing.
54	
55	## Device-side vs host-side
56	
57	```
58	  +----------------- dev loop (today) -----------------+
59	  |                                                    |
60	  |  laptop                  COR24 (emulator/FPGA)     |
61	  |  ------                  ---------------------     |
62	  |  vi src/foo.s  --UART->  sw-as24  --UART->  laptop |
63	  |  scripts/hex2bin.sh <----                          |
64	  |  cor24-run --load-binary foo.bin@addr --entry addr |
65	  |                                                    |
66	  +----------------------------------------------------+
67	
68	  +-------------- self-hosted loop (future) ------------+
69	  |                                                     |
70	  |  COR24 (single machine, no host)                    |
71	  |  -----------------------------                      |
72	  |  yocto-ed foo.s                                     |
73	  |  monitor: run sw-as24 < foo.s | sw-hexload @ 0x8000 |
74	  |  monitor: go 0x8000                                 |
75	  |                                                     |
76	  +-----------------------------------------------------+
77	```
78	
79	Same data flow. The laptop's role evaporates once the monitor and
80	the device-side utility are in place.
81	
82	## Proposed shape
83	
84	**Name (TBD):** `sw-hexload`. Source `src/sw-hexload.s`. Binary
85	`build/sw-hexload.bin`. Naming follows the ecosystem's `sw-` prefix
86	and the Unix-ish `hexload` verb.
87	
88	**Inputs:**
89	- UART RX: hex-ASCII stream. Two chars per byte, any case,
90	  tolerant of whitespace (space, tab, CR, LF) between pairs.
91	- Destination address: how to receive this is an open question;
92	  options in Section "Open questions".
93	
94	**Output:**
95	- A contiguous byte sequence starting at the destination address.
96	- Terminator semantics TBD: could be an explicit sentinel char
97	  (e.g., `.` or `!`), an EOT (0x04), or a length prefix. A
98	  sentinel keeps the stream purely character-oriented.
99	
100	**Non-goals (at least for first landing):**
101	- Checksums / framing (Intel HEX, S-record). Nice to have, not
102	  required for the self-hosting loop. Later saga.
103	- Multi-segment loads (non-contiguous regions). The first version
104	  handles one address range.
105	- Relocations. The sw-as24 output is already absolute-addressed;
106	  the loader just stores bytes verbatim.
107	- Automatic branch-to-dest after load. A separate monitor
108	  command (`go <addr>`) performs the transfer; keeping them
109	  orthogonal is safer and matches the Unix-pipeline model.
110	
111	## Sketch (not final)
112	
113	```
114	; src/sw-hexload.s (draft)
115	;
116	; Read hex-ASCII from UART RX until sentinel '.', decode each
117	; pair into a byte, store at consecutive memory addresses starting
118	; at $dest. Report the final length on UART TX so the caller knows
119	; how many bytes landed.
120	;
121	; Entry: r0 = destination address (abs24)
122	; Exit : r0 = number of bytes written
123	;        halts via self-branch or `jmp (r1)` return depending on
124	;        monitor calling convention (TBD).
125	
126	; pseudo-code outline -- exact register assignment pinned when
127	; this lands:
128	;
129	;   dest_ptr := r0
130	;   count    := 0
131	;   loop:
132	;     high_nibble <- getc
133	;     if high_nibble == '.': goto done
134	;     if high_nibble is whitespace: loop
135	;     low_nibble  <- getc
136	;     byte := hex_pair_to_byte(high_nibble, low_nibble)
137	;     mem[dest_ptr] := byte
138	;     dest_ptr += 1
139	;     count    += 1
140	;     goto loop
141	;   done:
142	;     r0 := count
143	;     return
144	```
145	
146	The `hex_pair_to_byte` helper is the real work: an ASCII digit
147	`'0'..'9'` maps to `0..9`, `'A'..'F'`/`'a'..'f'` to `10..15`. No
148	table needed; straight arithmetic + range checks.
149	
150	Interestingly, sw-as24 will need the *inverse* helper -- byte-to-hex
151	-- once it emits more than the two literals in saga 1. The two
152	helpers are duals; they can share bit-fiddling primitives even if
153	they live in different programs.
154	
155	## Open questions
156	
157	- **Q1. How does the loader receive its destination address?**
158	  Options: a UART preamble ("@addr\n<hex>.\n"), a compiled-in
159	  default overridden by monitor patch, or a subroutine calling
160	  convention where the monitor passes the address in a register.
161	  The subroutine form is cleanest once the monitor exists.
162	
163	- **Q2. Terminator semantics.** Sentinel char, EOT (0x04), length
164	  prefix, or a monitor-level EOF on the UART stream? Sentinel
165	  keeps the utility usable standalone from a human paste; length
166	  prefix is machine-friendlier. May support both.
167	
168	- **Q3. Error reporting.** What if the stream contains an invalid
169	  hex digit, or an odd number of digits, or runs past a safe
170	  memory range? Three-tier story probably: error code in r0,
171	  sentinel byte on UART, and partial-state left intact so the
172	  monitor can inspect.
173	
174	- **Q4. Relationship to a future host-monitor ABI.** Once
175	  `sw-cor24-monitor` exists (per the umbrella README under
176	  "Native tools"), sw-hexload may be a monitor built-in, a
177	  loadable utility, or a subroutine callable via a service
178	  vector. Decision deferred to the monitor saga.
179	
180	## Where this fits in the saga plan
181	
182	`sw-hexload` is **not** in the Relaunch saga (saga 1). The relaunch

16	## Current saga: 1. Relaunch

57	## Upcoming sagas (stubs)
58	
59	The ordering below is a current best guess. A stub may be split,
60	merged, or reordered when it becomes current.
61	
62	### 2. Lexer and line parsing
63	
64	Introduce real line parsing: comment stripping, label recognition,
65	mnemonic + operand tokenisation, numeric literal parsing
66	(decimal, hex, signed). Expands the recognised mnemonic set from 1
67	to a small handful (`nop`, `mov`, `push`, `pop`, plus whatever the
68	smoke tests need). Picks up **Q2** (static size limits) and
69	**Q3** (pass-1 caching) from `architecture.md`.
70	
71	### 3. Register + addressing mode parser
72	
73	Parse `r0..r2`, `fp`, `sp`, `z`, `iv`, `ir`, and the
74	`offset(base)` addressing form. Exercises the first real operand
75	decoding logic.
76	
77	### 4. Instruction encoding -- no-operand and register-only
78	
79	Encode every mnemonic that has only register (or no) operands.
80	Includes the ALU group (`add`, `sub`, `mul`, `and`, `or`, `xor`,
81	`shl`, `sra`, `srl`), the comparison group (`ceq`, `cls`, `clu`),
82	the extension group (`sxt`, `zxt`), and `mov` / `push` / `pop`.
83	
84	### 5. Instruction encoding -- immediates and loads
85	
86	Encode mnemonics that carry immediate operands or offset-base
87	addressing: `lc`, `lcu`, `la`, `lb`, `lbu`, `lw`, `sb`, `sw`.
88	
89	### 6. Symbol table and two-pass assembly
90	
91	Implement pass 1 (label collection + address assignment) and pass 2
92	(emission with known-symbol resolution). Forward references still
93	unsupported; they error rather than silently mis-encode.
94	
95	### 7. Forward references
96	
97	Record and patch forward references for branches (`rel8`) and `la`
98	(`abs24`). Report unresolved symbols and out-of-range branches.
99	
100	### 8. Branch / jump encoding
101	
102	Encode `bra`, `brt`, `brf`, `jmp`, `jal` (depending on operand form
103	and whether the symbol table already resolves the target from
104	saga 6 / 7 -- exact split pinned when this saga becomes current).
105	
106	### 9. Directives
107	
108	Implement `.org`, `.byte`, `.word`, `.comm`; silently accept the
109	no-op directives (`.text`, `.data`, `.globl`, `.align`).
110	
111	### 10. Error reporting polish
112	
113	Line numbers on every error, aggregation, non-zero exit on failure,
114	exhaustive error-category taxonomy. Tests with deliberately malformed
115	inputs.
116	
117	### 11. Hosting-model decision
118	
119	Resolve **Q1** from `architecture.md`. Choose standalone UART or
120	monitor-hosted and wire the chosen I/O path into `sw-as24.s`.
121	
122	### 12. Full ecosystem regression
123	
124	Collect `.s` inputs from `sw-cor24-forth`, `sw-cor24-macrolisp`,
125	`sw-cor24-pascal`, `sw-cor24-pcode`, and the emulator examples.
126	Assemble each with both `sw-as24` and `cor24-run`; require
127	byte-identical output. Fix divergences.
128	
129	### 13. Self-hosting close (G5)
130	
131	`sw-as24` assembles its own source (`src/sw-as24.s`) and the
132	resulting binary is byte-identical to `cor24-run`'s output for the
133	same source. Triggers the PRD's S3 success criterion.
134	
135	### 14. Release
136	
137	Tag a 0.1.0, write `CHANGES.md`, update
138	`sw-cor24-project/docs/status.md` to mark the native assembler
139	complete, and publish to GitHub. The vendor pin in `active.env`
140	moves from a local-path alias to a real `sw-cor24-x-assembler`
141	release artifact once one exists.
142

1	# Architecture -- `sw-as24`
2	
3	**Status:** Draft (Relaunch saga)
4	**Scope:** Long-lived reference. Evolves as sagas resolve the TBDs
5	flagged below.
6	
7	## 1. System context
8	
9	```
10	           .s text                         machine code
11	 (UART / monitor file) ───▶  sw-as24  ───▶ (UART / monitor file)
12	                              ▲
13	                              │ runs on
14	                              ▼
15	                  ┌──────────────────────┐
16	                  │ COR24 CPU            │
17	                  │ emulator  │ FPGA     │
18	                  └──────────────────────┘
19	```
20	
21	`sw-as24` is a COR24 program. Its source is COR24 `.s` and its binary
22	runs on the same ISA it assembles. During development, the vendored
23	`cor24-run` both assembles `sw-as24.s` (acting as the cross-assembler)
24	and executes the resulting binary (acting as the emulator). Once G5 is
25	met, `sw-as24` can assemble itself.
26	
27	## 2. Reference implementation
28	
29	The canonical behaviour is defined by `sw-cor24-x-assembler`
30	(`cor24-assembler` Rust crate, `rlib`). Its public entry point is
31	`Assembler::assemble(&str)`. This project ports that behaviour to `.s`.
32	
33	Encoding tables (opcodes, register numbers, ISA types) originate in
34	`sw-cor24-emulator`'s decode ROM; see `../sw-cor24-emulator` for the
35	authoritative definitions.
36	
37	## 3. Pipeline
38	
39	`sw-as24` is a two-pass assembler.
40	
41	```
42	 input ──▶ Pass 1: scan       ──▶  symbol table
43	                   (labels,
44	                    addresses)
45	                                  ──▶ instruction list
46	                                      (sized, symbols unresolved)
47	
48	 Pass 2: encode     ──▶ code buffer  ──▶ output
49	 (+ forward-ref
50	    patching)
51	```
52	
53	### Pass 1 -- address assignment
54	
55	- Read the input stream line by line.
56	- Strip comments (`;` to end of line).
57	- Recognise labels (identifier followed by `:`) and bind them to the
58	  current address in the symbol table.
59	- Compute the size of every instruction or directive -- *without*
60	  emitting code -- and advance the current address accordingly.
61	- Record enough state to replay the stream in pass 2 (either by
62	  re-reading the input or by caching a compact internal form; the
63	  choice is a design decision for a later saga).
64	
65	### Pass 2 -- code emission
66	
67	- Walk the stream (or the cached form) again.
68	- For every instruction, look up the opcode / encoding type in the
69	  mnemonic table and emit bytes into the code buffer.
70	- Resolve symbolic references: if the target label is known, patch
71	  immediately; if not, record a forward reference keyed by buffer
72	  offset, reference type (`rel8`, `abs24`), and label.
73	- After the second pass, walk the forward-reference list and patch
74	  the code buffer.
75	
76	### Error handling
77	
78	Errors do not abort; they are accumulated with a line number and
79	category (unknown mnemonic, invalid register, invalid operand,
80	unresolved symbol, branch out of range, duplicate label). After
81	assembly completes, the accumulated error list is written to the
82	output channel and the program exits non-zero.
83	
84	## 4. Data model (logical)
85	
86	Only parallel arrays -- the ISA has no struct literal semantics, and
87	the design constrains allocation to fixed, statically-sized regions.
88	
89	| Region             | Purpose                                             |
90	| ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------- |
91	| line buffer        | current input line                                  |
92	| token buffer       | mnemonic, operands for current line                 |
93	| mnemonic table     | names, encoding types, opcode bytes                 |
94	| symbol table       | `sym_names[]`, `sym_addrs[]`                        |
95	| forward refs       | `fwd_offsets[]`, `fwd_labels[]`, `fwd_types[]`      |
96	| code buffer        | emitted machine code                                |
97	| error log          | line numbers + error-kind codes                     |
98	
99	Exact sizes are a design concern (see `design.md`) and are pinned in
100	the first saga that cares about them.
101	
102	## 5. ISA coverage
103	
104	All mnemonics, addressing modes, and directives accepted by the Rust
105	cross-assembler. A coverage matrix (mnemonic → saga-introduced-in)
106	is maintained in `plan.md` as sagas progress.
107	
108	## 6. Hosting model (TBD)
109	
110	`sw-as24` must obtain input and emit output somehow. Two options are
111	under consideration; the relaunch saga is deliberately agnostic.
112	
113	**(i) Standalone, UART-only.** The program runs bare-metal. Input
114	bytes arrive on UART RX; output bytes go out on UART TX. A trivial
115	wrapper on the host (or `cor24-run`'s UART plumbing) feeds the input
116	and captures the output. This matches how `sw-cor24-forth` operates.
117	
118	**(ii) Monitor-hosted, service-vector I/O.** The program runs under
119	`sw-cor24-monitor` and uses service vectors for line-oriented I/O,
120	giving it something closer to stdin/stdout. This matches where the
121	ecosystem is heading for interactive tools.
122	
123	Trade-offs:
124	
125	- (i) minimises dependencies and works today; awkward for anything
126	  longer than a short `.s` file.
127	- (ii) matches the eventual on-device workflow (editor → assembler →
128	  run), but drags in the monitor and its ABI.
129	
130	The decision is deferred to the saga that first needs more than the
131	smoke-test's `nop` input. Until then, `sw-as24` reads from whatever
132	input channel `cor24-run` gives it and writes to whatever output
133	channel it gives it.
134	
135	## 7. Dependencies
136	
137	### Build-time (host)
138	
139	- `bash` -- build / test scripts, `vendor-fetch.sh`.
140	- `just` -- recipe runner.
141	- `jq` -- manifest parsing in `vendor-fetch.sh` (following the
142	  `sw-cor24-ocaml` precedent).
143	- Vendored `cor24-run` -- Rust-built binary, assembles `.s` → machine
144	  code and executes it. Pinned by commit in `vendor/active.env` +
145	  `vendor/sw-em24/<version>/version.json`.
146	
147	### Runtime (on COR24)
148	
149	- Today: none beyond the emulator's UART and the code itself.
150	- Eventually (hosting-model dependent): `sw-cor24-monitor` service
151	  vectors, if option (ii) is chosen.
152	
153	Explicitly *not* dependencies of this repository: Rust, `cargo`, any
154	C compiler, `make`, Python, any scripting runtime other than `bash`.
155	
156	## 8. Repository layout
157	
158	See `design.md` Section  Directory layout. The relaunch saga lays down this
159	structure; later sagas only fill it in.
160	
161	## 9. Compatibility contract
162	
163	`sw-as24`'s output is contractually byte-identical to
164	`cor24-run`'s output for every input both tools accept. This is the
165	one invariant the regression suite defends. Any divergence is a bug
166	in `sw-as24` (not a feature) until explicitly documented in this
167	architecture doc.
168	
169	## 10. Open questions (live)
170	
171	- **Q1.** Hosting model (Section 6). Owner: architecture saga after relaunch.
172	- **Q2.** Static size limits (code buffer, symbol table capacity,
173	  forward-ref capacity). Owner: the two-pass / symbol-table saga.
174	- **Q3.** Pass 1 caching strategy -- re-read the input vs. serialise a
175	  compact internal form. Owner: the two-pass saga.
176	- **Q4.** Self-hosting verification harness (G5). Owner: dedicated
177	  self-host saga near the end of the plan.

1	# PRD -- `sw-as24`, a self-hosting COR24 assembler
2	
3	**Status:** Draft (Relaunch saga)
4	**Owner:** Softwarewrighter
5	**Last updated:** 2026-04-18
6	
7	## Summary
8	
9	`sw-as24` is a COR24 assembler whose source is itself COR24 assembly. It
10	accepts standard COR24 `.s` input and emits machine code that is
11	byte-identical to the existing Rust cross-assembler. Once mature, the
12	assembler can assemble its own source on COR24 hardware, closing the
13	bootstrap loop and removing the host toolchain from the runtime path.
14	
15	## Why
16	
17	1. **Close the self-hosting loop.** The COR24 ecosystem has Forth, Lisp,
18	   Pascal, and others that all produce `.s`; none of them can *assemble*
19	   that `.s` on the target. The final missing piece is a native
20	   assembler.
21	2. **Reduce host dependencies.** Today every COR24 build requires a
22	   Rust toolchain via `cor24-run`. A native assembler lets a
23	   COR24-hosted monitor/editor workflow produce running binaries with
24	   no host involvement.
25	3. **Validate the ISA's self-hosting capacity.** Writing an assembler
26	   in `.s` stress-tests the ISA, exposes ergonomic gaps, and produces
27	   a worked example for every other low-level tool that follows.
28	4. **Educational.** A single-translation-unit assembler written in
29	   assembly is a tight, readable reference for how the ISA encodes
30	   instructions.
31	
32	## Goals
33	
34	- **G1.** Parse the full COR24 assembly grammar accepted by the Rust
35	  cross-assembler: all mnemonics, all addressing modes, directives
36	  (`.org`, `.byte`, `.word`, `.comm`), labels, comments, numeric
37	  literals (decimal, hex, signed).
38	- **G2.** Produce byte-identical machine code to the Rust cross-assembler
39	  for every `.s` input the Rust implementation accepts.
40	- **G3.** Implementation language is COR24 `.s` exclusively. Build
41	  glue is `bash` + `justfile`. No C, Rust, Python, or `make` shall
42	  be introduced into this repository.
43	- **G4.** Run on the COR24 emulator today and on COR24 FPGA hardware
44	  when hardware is available. The same `.s` source shall work on both.
45	- **G5.** Eventually self-host: `sw-as24` assembles its own source and
46	  produces a binary byte-identical to the one built by the Rust
47	  cross-assembler.
48	
49	## Non-goals
50	
51	- **N1.** Optimizing the emitted machine code. This is an assembler,
52	  not a peephole optimizer.
53	- **N2.** Supporting ISAs other than COR24.
54	- **N3.** Replacing `cor24-run` during *development*. The vendored
55	  cross-assembler remains the canonical reference and the fallback
56	  for validation until G5 is met.
57	- **N4.** Linker / loader functionality. `sw-as24` emits a single
58	  binary per input; multi-unit linking is out of scope (p-code has
59	  `p24-load` for that workflow).
60	- **N5.** New language extensions. If the Rust cross-assembler does
61	  not accept a syntax, `sw-as24` will not accept it either.
62	
63	## Success criteria
64	
65	- **S1.** `just test` passes: `tests/smoke/nop.s` assembled by
66	  `sw-as24` produces bytes byte-identical to `cor24-run`'s output.
67	- **S2.** (Future sagas) Regression suite of all `.s` files across
68	  `sw-cor24-forth`, `sw-cor24-macrolisp`, `sw-cor24-pascal`,
69	  `sw-cor24-pcode`, and the emulator examples assembles identically
70	  with both tools.
71	- **S3.** `sw-as24` assembled from its own source (by itself) produces
72	  a binary byte-identical to the one the Rust cross-assembler
73	  produces from the same `.s` source.
74	
75	## Stakeholders
76	
77	- Primary maintainer: Softwarewrighter (`dcasm` working tree).
78	- Downstream consumers: every language project under `sw-embed/` that
79	  emits `.s` and will eventually want to build on-device.
80	- Reference implementation: `sw-cor24-x-assembler` (Rust, rlib) and
81	  `sw-cor24-emulator`'s `cor24-run` binary.
82	
83	## Assumptions and constraints
84	
85	- **A1.** The Rust cross-assembler is the behavioural specification.
86	  Any ambiguity in COR24 syntax is resolved by deferring to whatever
87	  the Rust implementation does.
88	- **A2.** Until G5 is met, development relies on a locally-built
89	  vendored `cor24-run`. The vendor layout follows the pattern
90	  established in `sw-cor24-ocaml` (`vendor/<tool>/<version>/`
91	  with a manifest + gitignored `bin/`).
92	- **A3.** The runtime hosting model on COR24 (standalone UART I/O vs
93	  monitor-hosted via service vectors) is deliberately unresolved at
94	  relaunch time; see `architecture.md` Section  Hosting model.
95	- **A4.** ISA details -- mnemonic set, encoding, register file -- live
96	  in `sw-cor24-emulator` and `sw-cor24-x-assembler`. This project
97	  consumes them; it does not redefine them.
98	
99	## Out of scope for the Relaunch saga
100	
101	The relaunch saga produces scaffolding and a minimal `sw-as24.s` that
102	recognises the `nop` mnemonic and emits `0x00`. Everything else in
103	this PRD is addressed by later sagas enumerated in `plan.md`.

1	# Design -- Relaunch saga
2	
3	**Status:** Draft (Relaunch saga)
4	**Scope:** The scaffolding and first smoke test that prove the
5	pivot. This document is rewritten each phase; when the next saga
6	begins, prior phases' content moves to a dated section or is replaced.
7	
8	## Objective
9	
10	End the relaunch saga with a repository that (a) no longer describes a
11	C implementation, (b) builds its own `.s` source through a vendored
12	`cor24-run`, and (c) passes a smoke test that exercises the full
13	toolchain end-to-end on the single mnemonic `nop`.
14	
15	## Directory layout
16	
17	```
18	sw-cor24-assembler/
19	├── README.md                  # revamped, describes the .s-based scope
20	├── justfile                   # recipe runner
21	├── .gitignore                 # build/, vendor bin/
22	├── CLAUDE.md                  # updated session protocol + project rules
23	├── docs/
24	│   ├── prd.md
25	│   ├── architecture.md
26	│   ├── design.md              # this file
27	│   └── plan.md
28	├── src/
29	│   └── sw-as24.s              # the self-hosted assembler (starts tiny)
30	├── tests/
31	│   └── smoke/
32	│       └── nop.s              # single "nop"; expected byte 0x00
33	├── scripts/
34	│   ├── vendor-fetch.sh        # port of the sw-cor24-ocaml script
35	│   ├── build.sh               # assemble src/sw-as24.s with vendored cor24-run
36	│   └── test.sh                # assemble nop.s with both tools, diff bytes
37	└── vendor/
38	    ├── .gitignore             # ignores */v*/bin/*, keeps .gitkeep
39	    ├── active.env             # single-source-of-truth version pins
40	    └── sw-em24/<version>/
41	        ├── version.json       # manifest (repo, commit, artifact paths)
42	        └── bin/
43	            └── .gitkeep       # placeholder; cor24-run lands here on fetch
44	```
45	
46	The `.agentrail/` directory (new saga) and `.agentrail-archive/`
47	(preserved C-era saga) are tracked but are not part of the build
48	surface.
49	
50	## Vendor strategy
51	
52	Directly mirrors `sw-cor24-ocaml`. Every vendored tool has:
53	
54	1. An entry in `vendor/active.env`:
55	   ```
56	   SW_EM24_VERSION=v0.1.0
57	   ```
58	2. A manifest at `vendor/<tool>/<version>/version.json` with (at
59	   minimum) the upstream repo, a local sibling path, the pinned
60	   commit SHA, and a map of artifacts to copy.
61	3. A gitignored `bin/` (kept present by `.gitkeep`) where
62	   `scripts/vendor-fetch.sh` deposits the built binary.
63	4. A copy rule in `scripts/vendor-fetch.sh` that locates the local
64	   sibling repo via `repo_path_local`, verifies the pinned commit,
65	   and copies `target/release/cor24-run` into the manifest's `bin/`
66	   directory.
67	
68	The one vendored tool for the relaunch saga is `sw-em24` (upstream
69	`sw-cor24-emulator`, artifact `cor24-run`). The Rust cross-assembler
70	library (`sw-cor24-x-assembler`) is a transitive dependency of
71	`cor24-run` and is not vendored separately; if a later saga needs to
72	consult its source, it does so by path, not by copy. The user may
73	elect to re-vendor against a different upstream later; the version
74	pin (`active.env`) is the only thing that needs to change.
75	
76	## Justfile recipes
77	
78	Initial set -- minimum viable for the smoke test:
79	
80	| Recipe          | Purpose                                                  |
81	| --------------- | -------------------------------------------------------- |
82	| `vendor-fetch`  | Materialize `vendor/sw-em24/<v>/bin/cor24-run`.          |
83	| `vendor-check`  | Verify that expected vendor artifacts are present.       |
84	| `build`         | Assemble `src/sw-as24.s` with the vendored `cor24-run`.  |
85	| `test`          | Run the smoke test: both tools assemble `nop.s`; diff.   |
86	| `run FILE`      | Assemble + execute an arbitrary `.s` via `cor24-run`.    |
87	| `clean`         | Remove `build/`.                                         |
88	
89	Design decisions:
90	
91	- No `install` target -- this project builds artifacts under `build/`
92	  and does not install anywhere.
93	- `build` does not depend on `vendor-fetch`; failing fast with a
94	  helpful message when the vendor binary is missing is preferable
95	  to silent fetching.
96	- `test` is the contract the relaunch saga closes against. It must
97	  be green by the end of this saga.
98	
99	## Smoke test
100	
101	Input -- `tests/smoke/nop.s`:
102	
103	```
104	nop
105	```
106	
107	One line, no comments. Saga-1 sw-as24 deliberately does not parse
108	comments (step 008 non-goal); header comments return in saga 2
109	once comment-stripping lands.
110	
111	Expected behaviour:
112	
113	1. `cor24-run --assemble tests/smoke/nop.s build/ref.bin build/ref.lst`
114	   emits a single byte `0xFF` (the COR24 encoding of `nop` --
115	   verified empirically against the Rust assembler's listing
116	   output).
117	2. `cor24-run --load-binary build/sw-as24.bin@0 --entry 0 -u "nop\x04" ...`
118	   loads the already-assembled sw-as24 binary, feeds the .s content
119	   over UART RX (terminated with 0x04 EOT), runs until self-branch
120	   halt. sw-as24's UART TX carries the startup banner `S` followed
121	   by the hex representation of the emitted byte: `"SFF"` on match,
122	   `"S00"` on fail.
123	3. `scripts/test.sh` parses cor24-run's `UART output:` summary
124	   line, strips the one-char banner, pipes the remaining hex text
125	   through `scripts/hex2bin.sh`, and `diff -q`s the decoded
126	   `build/candidate.bin` against `build/ref.bin`. Exit 0 iff they
127	   match.
128	
129	### Why hex-encoded UART output
130	
131	sw-as24 emits its machine-code output as ASCII hex (two printable
132	chars per byte) rather than raw binary. Reason: cor24-run silently
133	filters byte `0x00` out of every UART-TX observation path -- the
134	per-byte log, the summary line, and even raw stdout under
135	`--terminal`. Verified by probing with `lc r0, 0` vs `lc r0, 1`:
136	the `0x01` byte survives to stdout, the `0x00` byte does not.
137	Since `nop` encodes to `0xFF` and future instructions will hit
138	`0x00` too, emitting raw bytes forfeits observability.
139	
140	Hex encoding sidesteps the filter (all hex digits are printable
141	0x30–0x46) and carries extra benefits: transport-robust over any
142	terminal, human-debuggable, matches the classical Intel-HEX /
143	S-record pattern for shipping binary over text channels. The
144	design holds even if cor24-run's filter is fixed upstream later.
145	
146	`scripts/hex2bin.sh` (a one-line wrapper around `xxd -r -p`) is the
147	host-side decoder. It pairs with sw-as24's output anywhere: the
148	smoke test, an FPGA flasher, a serial-console capture.
149	
150	## `src/sw-as24.s` for this saga
151	
152	Minimum viable assembler: recognises literally one mnemonic, `nop`,
153	and emits hex for one byte. Expectations:
154	
155	- UART banner `S` on startup (one printable char; test.sh strips).
156	- Three-byte UART read into r0, each byte compared against 'n',
157	  'o', 'p' in sequence via `ceq` + `brf fail`.
158	- On match, emit two UART bytes `F`, `F` (hex for `0xFF` = nop
159	  encoding). On mismatch, emit `0`, `0` (hex for `0x00` sentinel).
160	- Halt via branch-to-self (`halt: bra halt`). cor24-run detects
161	  self-branch and terminates the emulation.
162	
163	Calling convention for `putc` and `getc` helpers follows the
164	`jal r1, (r2)` pattern from `cor24-rs/rust-to-cor24/src/examples/`
165	(see `uart_hello.s`): `jal` stashes the return address in `r1`;
166	the callee pushes `r1` on entry, pops before `jmp (r1)` on exit,
167	leaving r1 free as scratch inside the subroutine.
168	
169	Deliberately omitted (later sagas): labels in input, comments in
170	input, multiple mnemonics, operands, directives, a symbol table,
171	two passes, proper byte-to-hex helper (currently hard-coded for
172	the two byte values this saga emits).
173	
174	## README revamp
175	
176	Replace the current C-oriented README with a scope statement that
177	matches the PRD:
178	
179	- One-line description: self-hosting COR24 assembler written in
180	  COR24 assembly.
181	- Build: `just vendor-fetch && just build`.
182	- Test: `just test`.

# sw-cor24-assembler — Claude Instructions

## Project Overview

Native COR24 assembler written in C. This assembler runs directly on
COR24 FPGA hardware, enabling self-hosted development without a host PC.

The C source is compiled by `tc24r` (the cross C compiler from
`sw-cor24-x-tinyc`) and assembled by `as24` (the cross-assembler from
`sw-cor24-x-assembler`). The resulting binary runs on COR24 hardware
or the emulator (`sw-cor24-emulator`).

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It contains your current step, prompt,
plan context, and any relevant skills/trajectories.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?". The step prompt IS your instruction.
Execute it directly.

### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
Commit your code changes with git. Use `/mw-cp` for the checkpoint
process (pre-commit checks, docs, detailed commit, push).

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
- If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
- If the saga is finished: add `--done`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make further code changes after running `agentrail complete`.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next
session. Future work belongs in the NEXT step, not this one.

## Key Rules

- **Do NOT skip steps** — the next session depends on accurate tracking
- **Do NOT ask for permission** — the step prompt is the instruction
- **Do NOT continue working** after `agentrail complete`
- **Commit before complete** — always commit first, then record completion

## Useful Commands

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state
agentrail history         # All completed steps
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context
```

## Build / Test

```bash
# Compile with tc24r (cross C compiler)
tc24r src/cas24.c -o cas24.s -I include/

# Assemble with as24 (cross-assembler)
# ... produces cas24.bin

# Run on emulator
cor24-run cas24.bin

# Test: assemble a .s file and compare output with cross-assembler
```

## Architecture

- Two-pass assembler matching the Rust cross-assembler's capabilities
- 49 mnemonics, symbol table, forward reference resolution
- Directives: .org, .byte, .word, .comm
- Input: COR24 `.s` assembly via UART
- Output: machine code bytes
- Written in C within tc24r's supported subset (no structs, no malloc)

## tc24r C Subset Constraints

- No structs — use parallel arrays
- No malloc/free — static allocation only
- No string library — hand-roll strcmp, strlen, etc.
- No varargs/printf — use direct UART output
- Single translation unit — one .c file (or #include chaining)
- 24-bit int, 8-bit char, pointers

## Cross-Repo Context

All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` as siblings:
- `sw-cor24-emulator` — emulator + ISA (foundation)
- `sw-cor24-x-assembler` — cross-assembler in Rust (reference implementation)
- `sw-cor24-x-tinyc` — cross C compiler in Rust (builds this project)
- `sw-cor24-assembler` — this repo (native assembler in C)

# sw-cor24-assembler -- Claude Instructions

## Project Overview

Self-hosting COR24 assembler written in COR24 assembly. Source is
`.s`, compiled by the vendored `cor24-run` Rust cross-assembler
during bootstrap, and targeted to run on COR24 hardware (emulator
today, FPGA eventually). Goal is byte-identical machine-code output
to `cor24-run --assemble`, then self-hosted assembly of its own
source once mature.

Full scope: [`docs/prd.md`](docs/prd.md). System structure and open
questions: [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md). Current
saga's design decisions: [`docs/design.md`](docs/design.md). Saga
roadmap: [`docs/plan.md`](docs/plan.md).

No C, Rust, Python, or `make` in this repository. Implementation is
`.s` plus `bash` + `justfile` + one-line shell helpers.

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It contains your current step, prompt,
plan context, and any relevant skills/trajectories.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?". The step prompt IS your instruction.
Execute it directly.

### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
Commit your code changes with git. Use `/mw-cp` for the checkpoint
process (pre-commit checks, docs, detailed commit, push).

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
- If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
- If the saga is finished: add `--done`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make further code changes after running `agentrail complete`.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next
session. Future work belongs in the NEXT step, not this one.

## Key Rules

- **Do NOT skip steps** -- the next session depends on accurate tracking
- **Do NOT ask for permission** -- the step prompt is the instruction
- **Do NOT continue working** after `agentrail complete`
- **Commit before complete** -- always commit first, then record completion

## Useful Commands

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state
agentrail history         # All completed steps
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context
```

## Build / Test

This project is driven by `just`. Recipes live in the top-level
`justfile`; discover them with `just --list`.

```bash
just vendor-fetch     # materialize vendor/sw-em24/<v>/bin/cor24-run
                      # from the sibling cor24-rs repo (or
                      # $SW_EM24_BIN, or system PATH -- see
                      # vendor/active.env for the override)
just build            # assemble src/sw-as24.s via vendored cor24-run
                      # -> build/sw-as24.bin + build/sw-as24.lst
just test             # byte-identical smoke test: cor24-run and
                      # sw-as24 must agree on tests/smoke/nop.s
just run FILE         # assemble + execute a user .s via cor24-run
just clean            # remove build/
```

Exit criterion for the current saga is `just test` green against
`tests/smoke/nop.s`.

## Architecture (summary)

sw-as24 is a two-pass COR24 assembler (pass 1 scans labels +
addresses, pass 2 emits code + patches forward references). ISA
coverage mirrors the Rust cross-assembler. Byte-identical output
is the one invariant the regression suite defends.

UART output is hex-ASCII, not raw binary: `cor24-run` filters
`0x00` from every observation path, so sw-as24 emits two printable
hex chars per byte. `scripts/hex2bin.sh` is the host-side decoder;
[`docs/utility.md`](docs/utility.md) sketches a future on-device
decoder (`sw-hexload`) that closes the self-hosting loop.

For the full system diagram, pipeline, data model, and the four
open questions (hosting model, static size limits, pass-1 caching,
self-host verification), read [`docs/architecture.md`](docs/architecture.md).

## Cross-Repo Context

Siblings referenced by this project (paths vary per dev system --
this tree is `/disk1/.../work/dcasm/github/sw-embed/`):

- [`cor24-rs`](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) -- stabilized
  upstream that ships `cor24-run` (Rust cross-assembler + emulator).
  Pinned by commit in `vendor/sw-em24/v0.1.0/version.json`.
- [`sw-cor24-emulator`](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator)
  -- sw-embed fork of `cor24-rs` reserved for future divergence.
- [`sw-cor24-x-assembler`](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler)
  -- Rust cross-assembler library (rlib), behavioural reference.
- [`sw-cor24-project`](https://github.com/sw-embed/sw-cor24-project)
  -- ecosystem umbrella listing every COR24 repo.

See `vendor/active.env` for how this project discovers the
vendored `cor24-run` binary (sibling source tree, `$SW_EM24_BIN`
env override, or system PATH).

# sw-cor24-assembler — Claude Instructions

## Project Overview

Native COR24 assembler written in C. This assembler runs directly on
COR24 FPGA hardware, enabling self-hosted development without a host PC.

The C source is compiled by `tc24r` (the cross C compiler from
`sw-cor24-x-tinyc`) and assembled by `as24` (the cross-assembler from
`sw-cor24-x-assembler`). The resulting binary runs on COR24 hardware
or the emulator (`sw-cor24-emulator`).

## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)

### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
```bash
agentrail next
```
Read the output carefully. It contains your current step, prompt,
plan context, and any relevant skills/trajectories.

### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
```bash
agentrail begin
```

### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
Do NOT ask "want me to proceed?". The step prompt IS your instruction.
Execute it directly.

### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
Commit your code changes with git. Use `/mw-cp` for the checkpoint
process (pre-commit checks, docs, detailed commit, push).

### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
```bash
agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
  --reward 1 \
  --actions "tools and approach used"
```
- If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
- If the saga is finished: add `--done`

### 6. STOP (after complete, DO NOT continue working)
Do NOT make further code changes after running `agentrail complete`.
Any changes after complete are untracked and invisible to the next
session. Future work belongs in the NEXT step, not this one.

## Key Rules

- **Do NOT skip steps** — the next session depends on accurate tracking
- **Do NOT ask for permission** — the step prompt is the instruction
- **Do NOT continue working** after `agentrail complete`
- **Commit before complete** — always commit first, then record completion

## Useful Commands

```bash
agentrail status          # Current saga state
agentrail history         # All completed steps
agentrail plan            # View the plan
agentrail next            # Current step + context
```

## Build / Test

```bash
# Compile with tc24r (cross C compiler)
tc24r src/cas24.c -o cas24.s -I include/

# Assemble with as24 (cross-assembler)
# ... produces cas24.bin

# Run on emulator
cor24-run cas24.bin

# Test: assemble a .s file and compare output with cross-assembler
```

## Architecture

- Two-pass assembler matching the Rust cross-assembler's capabilities
- 49 mnemonics, symbol table, forward reference resolution
- Directives: .org, .byte, .word, .comm
- Input: COR24 `.s` assembly via UART
- Output: machine code bytes
- Written in C within tc24r's supported subset (no structs, no malloc)

## tc24r C Subset Constraints

- No structs — use parallel arrays
- No malloc/free — static allocation only
- No string library — hand-roll strcmp, strlen, etc.
- No varargs/printf — use direct UART output
- Single translation unit — one .c file (or #include chaining)
- 24-bit int, 8-bit char, pointers

## Cross-Repo Context

All COR24 repos live under `~/github/sw-embed/` as siblings:
- `sw-cor24-emulator` — emulator + ISA (foundation)
- `sw-cor24-x-assembler` — cross-assembler in Rust (reference implementation)
- `sw-cor24-x-tinyc` — cross C compiler in Rust (builds this project)
- `sw-cor24-assembler` — this repo (native assembler in C)
